does: This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of machine unlearning by establishing formal complexity bounds and trade-offs between utility, time, and space requirements. The work distinguishes between in-distribution and out-of-distribution forget data, showing that simple empirical risk minimization with output perturbation achieves tight complexity bounds for in-distribution settings while revealing fundamental limitations for out-of-distribution cases. The authors provide certification guarantees analogous to differential privacy, closing previous theoretical gaps and establishing when unlearning can be efficient versus when it may require retraining complexity.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in machine unlearning by moving beyond heuristic approaches toward formal theoretical foundations. It connects to differential privacy research by examining the relationship between unlearning and privacy guarantees, clarifying both the connections and separations between these frameworks. The paper extends prior work on the computational complexity of machine learning by introducing unlearning-specific complexity measures and responding to open questions about whether unlearning can be fundamentally cheaper than retraining. It situates itself within broader efforts to make post-deployment model modification theoretically sound and practically viable.
enables: This theoretical framework enables more principled design of unlearning algorithms by clearly delineating what is and isn't possible under different data distributions. The complexity characterizations provide guidance for practitioners about when efficient unlearning is feasible versus when alternative strategies are necessary. The work opens directions for developing specialized unlearning procedures tailored to out-of-distribution scenarios and points toward hybrid approaches combining efficient methods for in-distribution forget sets with strategies for handling distribution shift. These foundations could support future work on certified unlearning systems with formal privacy and utility guarantees for real-world deployment contexts.
